EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry vegetation combined with a brisk breeze created favorable conditions for major brush fire.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire scorched four acres of grassland near HaPenny on the island's south shore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry conditions persisted across Saint Croix during the month of March.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire scorched 40 acres at Estate Granard.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry weather across Saint Croix promotes brush fires.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brush fire scorched more than a 100 acres in an open field in Estate Concordia east of Fredericksted.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dry vegetation conditions due to lack of rainfall in St Croix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large brush fire burnt more than 800 acres near Castle Nugent, Lowry Hill and Estate Sight on Saint Croix's East End.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A tropical wave moving across the U.S. Virgin Islands during the morning produced showers and thunderstorms. Minor urban and road flooding was reported in Saint Croix.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One section of Melvin Evans Highway adjacent to Carino Water was reported flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough east of the Leeward islands combined with the remnants of Tropical Storm Karen and a developing low pressure system northwest of Puerto Rico to produce showers and thunderstorms across the U.S. Virgin Islands on Tuesday October 2nd. Isolated sections of Saint Croix received up to two inches of rainfall. The interaction of the low level disturbance over the northern Leeward Islands and the low pressure system northwest of Puerto Rico continued throughout the week, generating showers and thunderstorms across the U.S. Virgin Islands on Thursday October 4th. Minor flooding problems were reported in some areas of Saint Croix and Saint John.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rain were reported at the Saint Croix airport. Doppler radar indicated a strong thunderstorm with very heavy rain in the vicinity and over the island of Saint Croix.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed south of Saint Croix during the weekend of October 27-28 moved away from the U.S. Virgin Islands and eventually became Tropical Storm Noel when it was located along Haiti's south coast. A trough of low pressure over the northern Leeward Islands combined with the tail weather of Noel and produced significant rainfall across Saint Croix. Doppler radar 3 day rainfall estimates from Thursday through Sunday were in the order of 8 to 10 inches. NWS spotters from the west and southwest end of Saint Croix reported from 7 to 8 inches for the same period. However the greatest rainfall accumulations occurred between Thursday and Saturday morning. Gusty winds generated by the associated showers and thunderstorms blew down a few tress on Saint Croix, and caused some power outages. The Virgin Islands Territorial Emergency Management Agency reported some rock slides on roads in Saint John, Saint Thomas experienced flooding at Mandela Circle and Havensight area, some mudslides in Tabor and Harmony, and some downed trees in Scott Free. Several roads were flooded out across Saint Croix Friday night. Access roads to Grove Place, Williams Delight, and sectors of Queen Mary Highway were reported impassable.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Periods of heavy rainfall affected the Saint Croix airport.

CZ_NAME: ST. THOMAS / ST. JOHN AND ADJACENT ISLANDS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On December 10, 2007, a persistent area of low pressure just north Saint Thomas, U.S. Virgin Islands, rapidly developed into Subtropical storm Olga. Olga was the 10th tropical cyclone to develop during the month of December in roughly 150 years of records. Additionally, it is only the 4th December tropical storm on record to hit land. Impacts across the U.S. Virgin Islands...Saint Thomas only received 0.97��� of precipitation at the airport. Several minor power outages were reported throughout St. Thomas and St. Croix, caused by tree limbs brushing against power lines. Some local businesses, including a charter flight company, were disrupted during the height of the storm, but resumed normal activities as the weather improved.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Squall lines associated with the outer bands of a well defined disturbance that later became Sub-tropical Olga produced tropical storm force wind gusts across Saint Thomas. Several power outages were reported across the territory.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April began with dry weather but a wet pattern slowly emerged over the region by mid-April which led to lots of heavy rainfall towards the end of the month.  A near stationary weather pattern was to blame for the longevity of the event as a mid to upper level trough remained northwest of the local area. This upper level feature in combination with a surface trough, a moist and unstable air mass and local effects resulted in the development of showers and thunderstorms across the local islands with the bulk of the rainfall in the Virgin Islands. The end result was moderate localized flooding in the Virgin Islands. In St. Thomas and St. John in the U.S. Virgin Islands, the heavy rain and flooding forced school closings. St. Thomas airport recorded the greatest 24 hour rainfall accumulation of 3.40 inches on 23-24, and rainfall for the month was 3.92 inches above normal. Numerous flooded areas were reported  on St. Thomas due to the heavy rains of Sunday April 22 and Monday April 23. St. John reported some flooded areas, but no major flooding problems were reported on St. Croix, although several homes in the La Grande Princesse area experienced flooding of yards and driveways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain was reported at the St. Thomas airport.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain was reported at the St. Thomas airport. As a result of the heavy rains of the last two days localized flooding was reported on areas of St Thomas like Raphune Hill, Mandela Circle, Donoe Gut, Magens Bay Road, St. Peter Mountain Road, Four Corner's intersection, Estate Hope, Fortuna Road, Tabor and Harmony, Hidden valley, Smith Bay Road, Fort Mylner, Frenchtown Gas Station Gut, Denmark Hill, Coki Point Road and the roadway in Nadir.  On St. John flooded areas included Centerline Road from Cruz Bay to the intersection of Route 107 in Coral Bay, parts of North Shore Road, and roadways in downtown Cruz Bay.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dean became tropical depression four on the morning of August 13th when it was about 2000 miles east of the Lesser Antilles. On August 14th the depression increased its strength and was upgraded to Tropical Storm Dean when it was located about 1490 miles east of the Lesser Antilles. The storm continued to intensify and on August 16th at 5 am was upgraded to hurricane status, with maximum winds of 75 mph. Then it was located about 590 miles east of Martinique. At 5 pm on August 16th, a tropical storm watch was issued for Puerto Rico and the U.S Virgin Islands, at 11 pm, the tropical storm watch for Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands was upgraded to a  tropical storm warning. Dean continued to intensify during Friday August 17th,and by 2 am Saturday August 18th, its maximum sustained winds had increased to near 150 mph. It maintained its westward track through Saturday morning, when at 11 am the  tropical storm warning for the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico was discontinued. Most of local effects associated with the passage of Hurricane Dean south of the U.S. Virgin Islands and Puerto Rico were in association with the extreme outer rain bands along the northern part of the hurricane and the extensive radius of sustained tropical storm force winds. Sustained tropical storm-force winds likely occurred over most of the Saint Croix and Puerto Rico near shore waters. Although no sustained tropical storm force winds were officially observed in any of the sensors at the airports in Saint Croix and Puerto Rico, tropical storm force winds gusts were observed. Very likely the higher elevations of Saint Croix and Puerto Rico experienced sustained tropical storm force winds. The maximum ASOS-measured peak wind gusts included 46 mph at the Saint Croix airport, 41 mph at the Saint Thomas Airport, 44 mph at Ceiba Airport, and 36 mph at the Carolina Airport. Other unofficial wind gust measurements included 59 mph at the NOAA Crews Salt River Bay Station in Saint Croix, 58 mph at Hess Oil Refinery, and 46 mph at the NOAA Crews Station at La Parguera, in Lajas, Puerto Rico. Damage was limited to fallen trees, utility lines downed by wind gusts Friday night. Some coastal access roads around Saint Croix sustained moderate to higher damage due to beach erosion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tropical storm force wind gusts of 46 mph were recorded at the Henry E. Rohlsen airport in Saint Croix. The NOAA CREWS Station at Salt River Bay Saint Croix recorded a wind gust of 59 mph from the southeast at 6 pm on August 17th. The wind gusts blew down some banana and plantain trees around south shore.
